advaita.english: Those whose very nature is desire (kāmātmānaḥ —

) hold heaven as the highest end, proclaiming speech laden with rites that yields only rebirth as its fruit. Śaṅkara cuts to the structural diagnosis: such speech is seductive precisely because it multiplies specific rites (kriyā-viśeṣa-bahulā) aimed at pleasure and lordship, keeping the fool rotating in saṃsāra (ghaṭī-yantra-vad — like a water-wheel) without once approaching jñāna. The very elaborateness of the karmakāṇḍa is the trap: the more rites it advertises, the more it masks the singular inquiry that could end rebirth.
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara reads the entire verse as a diagnosis of why the ordinary Vedic mind cannot attain vyavasāyātmikā buddhi. The rites themselves are not condemned as false — they are condemned as insufficient. The goal is dissolution of the desiring subject, not its satisfaction via heaven.

viśiṣṭādvaita.english: Rāmānuja foregrounds the phrase anapāramya (all-at-once pleasingness) embedded in his reading of puṣpitā vāk: this speech is beautiful like a flower — showy, fragrant, but bearing fruit only of the perishable kind (janma-karma-phala). Those who are kāmapravana-manasaḥ (minds bent toward desire) and who read the Veda's heaven-texts as the Veda's total intention have simply not read far enough — they have frozen at the outer petals and missed the seed. Such speech circles back to rebirth-action (punar-janma-karmākhya-phala) and does not open toward Bhagavān's service.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Where Śaṅkara locates the error in the desiring subject's nature, Rāmānuja locates it in an incomplete reading of scriptural intention. The Veda itself, read fully, points toward Bhagavān as the real phala; the kāmī (desiring person) has simply stopped reading too soon.

dvaita.english: Madhva's commentary is characteristically sparse and polemical: those who assert that the Vedas exist solely to deliver bhogaiśvarya (enjoyment and lordship) are making a categorical error about the Veda's very purpose. For Madhva, Hari alone is svatantra (independently real); the jīva is paratantra (dependently real). Any reading of Vedic rites that makes enjoyment the terminal goal has substituted the jīva's craving for Hari's supremacy. The specific rites (kriyā-viśeṣāḥ) serve Hari when performed in dependence; performed for svarga they become instruments of further bondage.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva does not elaborate a phenomenology of desire; he delivers a structural correction: the Veda's telos is Hari-worship, not svarga. His terseness is itself a rhetorical act — there is nothing more to say once the telos is corrected.

śuddhādvaita.english: Vallabha parses the compound kāmātmānaḥ unusually precisely — kāmayate ātmā yeṣām:

— making desire not an attribute but the whole constitution of the person. These are not people who happen to have desires; they are people constituted as desiring-selves. Such persons mistake the Veda's outermost fruit-yielding speech (janma-karma-phala-pradā vāk) for the Veda's heart, while remaining oblivious to the Veda's real tātparya (intention). In the Puṣṭi-mārga reading, Kṛṣṇa's prasāda alone dissolves this constitution; the craving-self cannot uproot itself through more rites.
key_terms_preserved: kāmayate ātmā yeṣām (whose self is the very one that desires)
veda-vāda-tātparya-anabhijña (ignorant of the Veda's true intention)
svarga-loka eva paraḥ (only svarga as the highest)
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha's distinctive move is the grammatical one: making desire structural to selfhood, not incidental. This sets up the Puṣṭi-mārga insistence that only Kṛṣṇa's grace can dissolve what no practice can overcome from within.

bhakti.english: — and links agitation directly to the elevation of svarga as the highest puruṣārtha (end of human life). The speech they propagate is not merely misguided but positively productive of rebirth: janma-karma-phala-pradā means it delivers fresh bodies, fresh actions, fresh consequences in an unbroken chain. Śrīdhara balances Advaita and bhakti inflections: the diagnosis is about misdirected cognition (kāma-ākulatā disturbs discernment), but the implicit remedy is the steady bhakti that alone quiets the agitation at its root.
key_terms_preserved: svarga eva paraḥ puruṣārthaḥ (only heaven as the highest goal)
bhogaiśvarya-prāpti-sādhanabhūtā (serving as the means to enjoyment-lordship)
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara's voice is more affectively engaged than Śaṅkara's: he describes agitation (ākulatā) not merely structural error. The remedy is implied rather than stated — balanced bhakti that calms rather than inflames.

advaita-bhakti.english: Madhusūdana reads 2.42–2.44 as a single diagnostic unit and makes the water-wheel image explicit and clinical: the rites deliver birth, fresh action, and fruit (janma-karma-phala) in a ghāṭī-yantra-like unbroken revolution, where even heavenly enjoyment — the nectar-drinking and Urvaśī-sports of svarga — is perishable (vinaśvara). The karmakāṇḍa is

(vastly more extensive) than the jñānakāṇḍa, but that very extensiveness marks its inadequacy: what is wide is not deep. He then takes the synthetic turn: kāmya rites performed without phala-abhisandhi (result-intention) do purify, but bhoga-ānukūla śuddhi (pleasure-conducive purification) cannot support jñāna. Only niṣkāma action — which for Madhusūdana is action saturated with Kṛṣṇa-bhakti — produces the purification that opens into ahaṃ brahmā iti avasthānam (the settled cognition
key_terms_preserved: ahaṃ brahmā iti avasthānam (settled cognition: I am Brahman)
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana is the only panelist who explicitly distinguishes two kinds of purification — bhoga-conducive and jñāna-conducive — and names the failure mechanism precisely. This is the synthetic contribution: not all rites are equally obstructive; the variable is phala-abhisandhi, and bhakti is what dissolves it.

so_what_questions: If the Vedas themselves authorize the speech being condemned here, what distinguishes legitimate Vedic ritual prescription from the
Kṛṣṇa criticizes — is the difference in the speaker's intention, the listener's understanding, or the text's telos?

The verse pairs kāmātmānaḥ (desire-natured) with svargaparāḥ (heaven-focused): does the text suggest heaven is merely a low goal, or that the very structure of goal-directed desire is the problem regardless of how elevated the goal?
Madhusūdana's water-wheel image (ghāṭī-yantra) implies the rite-rebirth cycle is mechanically self-sustaining — what would it mean to interrupt a mechanism rather than simply to desire to leave it?
) describes a pathology of elaboration — does this imply a general principle that spiritual sophistication expressed as procedural complexity is suspect?
Janma-karma-phala-pradā names rebirth itself as the fruit of these rites — how does this reframe conventional religious achievement (performing complex rituals correctly) as spiritual failure?
The verse's addressee is implicitly a Vedic practitioner already committed to ritual — what does it mean for a tradition to critique its own practitioners from within its own highest text?

Vallabha reads kāmātmānaḥ as structural selfhood-constituted-by-desire rather than a person who incidentally has desires — what practical difference does this ontological distinction make for what kind of remedy is possible?

advaita.application: Notice when your spiritual or professional planning accumulates elaborate procedures — new courses, certifications, side-projects — while the felt sense of insufficiency that drives them remains untouched. Śaṅkara's diagnosis of kriyā-viśeṣa-bahulā (thickness of specific rites) maps directly onto the proliferation of productivity systems, credential-seeking, or wellness routines that promise transformation but only produce the next version of the same desiring self. The Advaita practice is to periodically ask not 'what should I add?' but 'what am I circling around that I have not yet looked at directly?'

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Rāmānuja's image of the practitioner who stops at the Veda's

applies to any situation where you follow a practice's most visible, socially legible layer — attending services, performing visible charity, meeting formal obligations — without engaging its deeper intention, which is reorientation of the whole person toward Bhagavān. The everyday test is whether your practice is making you more porous to others' reality (kainkarya, service-readiness) or whether it is primarily managing your self-image as a religious person. Stop reading too soon and the flower never opens into fruit.

dvaita.application: Madhva's structural correction translates as: identify the real telos behind your actions. When you work hard, serve your community, or practice devotion, the operative question is whose glory the action is ultimately organized around — yours (svarga-parā, outcome-obsessed) or Hari's (service-structured). This is not a sentimental adjustment but an ontological one: the jīva is constitutionally dependent (paratantra), and actions that proceed from the fiction of independence produce the bondage the verse names. Concretely: take one recurring action and ask whether its 'success' condition is defined by your satisfaction or by fidelity to something larger.

śuddhādvaita.application: Vallabha's reading that kāmātmānaḥ names desire as one's very constitution — not a bad habit but the organizing principle of the self — implies that no quantity of effortful practice can undo it from within. The practical implication is to stop treating spiritual development primarily as self-improvement and start treating it as a receptivity practice: creating conditions in which Kṛṣṇa's prasāda (grace-gift) can arrive. In daily life this looks like deliberately reducing the transactional frame around devotion — bhajana, service, study — removing the implicit 'in order that' from these acts and simply doing them in Kṛṣṇa's presence.

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's kāmākula-citta (agitated mind) is recognizable as the background hum of goal-directedness that makes genuine rest impossible — always planning the next move, qualifying the present moment by future outcome. The bhakti application is to notice that agitation (ākulatā) is the actual obstacle, not merely a symptom, and that bhakti practice is specifically designed to address the affective level where Advaita analysis cannot easily reach. This means making devotional practice — recitation, singing, offering — a direct counterweight to agitation rather than one more item on a to-do list, and measuring the practice not by its productivity but by its actual calming effect on the mind's surface.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's distinction between bhoga-ānukūla śuddhi (purification that feeds pleasure-seeking) and jñāna-upayoginī śuddhi (purification that supports liberating insight) offers a diagnostic for evaluating one's current practices. Ask of any spiritual practice, self-care routine, or growth activity: does this leave me cleaner and quieter, or does it leave me wanting the next version? Practices organized around outcome-desire (phala-abhisandhi) tend to produce the first kind of purification at best — more refined desire — while practice saturated with Kṛṣṇa-bhakti and released from result-fixation produces the second. The practical move is to identify one practice where you can genuinely release the result-expectation and observe what shifts.

self_assessment.anchoring: All six renderings draw directly from the bhāṣya prose in the payload — Śaṅkara's ghaṭī-yantra image, Rāmānuja's puṣpitā/anapāramya reading, Madhva's terse telos-correction, Vallabha's grammatical kāmayate-ātmā parsing, Śrīdhara's kāmākula-cittāḥ framing, Madhusūdana's vinaśvara/bhoga-ānukūla distinction. No doctrinal content was imported from generic knowledge without a textual basis in the supplied commentary.
self_assessment.divergence_quality: Genuine doctrinal difference is preserved: Śaṅkara diagnoses structural desire; Rāmānuja locates incomplete scriptural reading; Madhva delivers telos-correction; Vallabha makes desire constitutional to selfhood; Śrīdhara centers affective agitation; Madhusūdana distinguishes two kinds of purification. These are not six versions of the same point.
self_assessment.constraint_compliance: Sanskrit terms appear only in Sanskrit form with parenthetical English glosses. No English suffixes attached to Sanskrit roots. Doctrinal_renderings is a dict keyed by school name, not a list. Everyday applications are school-keyed, 2–3 sentences each, distinct in register and practical advice.
self_assessment.limitations: Dvaita's bhāṣya in the payload is very sparse (one sentence); the Dvaita rendering draws on the structural logic of that sentence but necessarily supplements from the school's known doctrinal architecture (svatantra/paratantra). This is flagged rather than hidden. Bhakti panel entry contained JavaScript artifact text mixed into the commentary; the rendering was extracted from the Sanskrit portions only.
